article_title: Stanford and Arc Institute Trace Age-Related Memory Loss to a Single Gut Bacterium, Then Reverse It via the Vagus Nerve

content_preview.title: Stanford and Arc Institute Trace Age-Related Memory Loss to a Single Gut Bacterium, Then Reverse It via the Vagus Nerve
content_preview.summary: A Nature study identifies Parabacteroides goldsteinii as a driver of cognitive decline in aging mice and shows that stimulating the vagus nerve with GLP-1 receptor agonists restores memory to youthful levels.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Researchers at Stanford Medicine and the Arc Institute have identified a specific gut bacterium that accumulates with age, triggers inflammation along the vagus nerve, and impairs memory formation in the hippocampus. Critically, the team demonstrated that stimulating the vagus nerve in aged mice restored their cognitive performance to levels indistinguishable from young animals. The findings were [published in Nature on March 11, 2026](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-026-1019...
